items.q: What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559?
items.correct: A deliberate via media — a Protestant doctrine and English-language prayer book combined with some Catholic-style ceremony — designed to maximise conformity.
items.misconception: Elizabeth made England fully Protestant and banned Catholic worship outright.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth reunited England with the Pope to make peace with Spain.
items.why: The settlement was a compromise designed to be broadly acceptable. Students assume binary Protestant/Catholic divides; Elizabeth deliberately blurred them to keep her realm stable.

items.q: Why did Britain win the Battle of Britain?
items.correct: Radar, the Dowding command system, home advantage in pilot recovery, German tactical errors (switching to bombing cities), and adequate fighter production.
items.misconception: The RAF won because Spitfires were better than any German aircraft.
items.random_wrong: Hitler called off the attack to invade the USSR immediately.
items.why: The Bf 109 was comparable to the Spitfire. Victory came from integrated air defence and German strategic mistakes, not aircraft superiority — a key point in modern historiography.

items.q: What did Magna Carta actually do in 1215?
items.correct: It was a peace treaty between King John and rebel barons protecting baronial privileges and limiting arbitrary royal taxation and imprisonment — it was annulled within months.
items.misconception: Magna Carta gave ordinary English people democratic rights and freedom.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta abolished the monarchy and created Parliament.
items.why: Magna Carta protected the elite, not commoners; villeins are barely mentioned. Its democratic reputation is a later (17th-century) reinterpretation projected backwards.

items.q: Which 1601 law made parishes responsible for their poor?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Poor Law. “Statute of Artificers” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which great Elizabethan country house was built by Bess of Hardwick?
items.why: Correct answer: Hardwick Hall. “Hampton Court” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many million people lived in England by 1600?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
